card.title: Why Bread Rises
card.teaser: Every loaf is a quiet collaboration between a fungus and a protein — and neither one knows you exist.
card.coverImagePrompt: A loaf of sourdough cross-sectioned showing airy open crumb, steam rising, warm bakery light, flour dust in the air, cinematic food photography

lessons.title: Trapped farts
lessons.body: That pillowy hole-filled crumb in a good baguette is, at the atomic level, a vast network of frozen gas bubbles. Specifically: carbon dioxide exhaled by microscopic fungi and held in place by a stretchy protein web. Humans have been eating bread for at least 14,500 years — archaeologists found charred crumbs in a Jordanian hearth from well before farming. For most of that history, nobody knew yeast was alive.

visual.imagePrompt: Extreme close-up of bread crumb showing thousands of tiny air pockets, each glowing softly, macro lens

lessons.title: The fungus and the glue
lessons.body: Two things make bread rise. First: yeast — specifically Saccharomyces cerevisiae, a single-celled fungus that eats sugar and burps CO₂. Second: gluten, a protein formed when two flour proteins (glutenin and gliadin) meet water and get kneaded. Glutenin gives strength; gliadin gives stretch. Together they form an elastic mesh, strong enough to trap each yeast burp without popping.

keyTerms.definition: A single-celled fungus that ferments sugars into CO₂ and ethanol
A stretchy protein network formed when glutenin and gliadin in wheat flour meet water and are worked together

visual.imagePrompt: A microscopic view of gluten strands forming a stretchy web with yeast cells trapped inside, exhaling bubbles

lessons.title: The slow breath of dough
lessons.body: At room temperature, a well-fed yeast cell produces carbon dioxide every few seconds. Over a two-hour rise, a trillion yeast cells in an average loaf might release roughly a liter of gas. That gas inflates the gluten web. During kneading, you align and strengthen the protein strands; during proofing, you give the yeast time to puff them. Skip either step and the loaf is dense.
lessons.funFact: The alcohol yeast produces doesn't disappear — bread dough is mildly alcoholic. It just evaporates in the oven, leaving behind some of the flavor compounds.

visual.imagePrompt: A slow-motion timelapse of dough rising in a bowl, a cutaway showing the interior growing bubbles over hours

lessons.title: Oven spring, the final act
lessons.body: When the loaf hits a 220°C oven, three things happen in fast succession. The trapped CO₂ expands (warm gas takes more space). Water turns to steam, adding more lift. The yeast, before dying around 60°C, gives a last panicked burst of fermentation. This is 'oven spring' — the dramatic final rise in the first ten minutes of baking. After that, starch gelatinizes, proteins set, and the crumb is locked in forever.

visual.imagePrompt: A loaf of bread visibly swelling in a glass-front oven, steam rising, crust beginning to form, golden lighting

lessons.title: A 14,000-year contract
lessons.body: Bread is essentially a domesticated microorganism working inside a protein scaffold, released from a grain you grew from a grass. Every loaf is a quiet agreement across kingdoms — fungus, plant, and human — built on three ingredients: flour, water, time. Master that triangle and you can make any bread in the world.

quiz.question: What gas makes bread rise?
quiz.explanation: Yeast metabolizes sugars into carbon dioxide and ethanol; the CO₂ is what inflates the dough.

quiz.question: What two proteins combine to form gluten?
quiz.explanation: Glutenin provides strength, gliadin provides stretch — together with water they form the elastic gluten network.

quiz.question: What is 'oven spring'?
options: The sudden cooling when you open the oven
The rapid final rise in the first minutes of baking
The sound a loaf makes when done
quiz.explanation: In the first ~10 minutes, gas expansion, steam, and a final yeast burst dramatically inflate the loaf.

sources.title: Archaeobotanical evidence reveals the origins of bread 14,400 years ago in northeastern Jordan
